title: Iran VCR (Videocassette) and Satellite Dish Ownership/Ban History, 1983-2016
source_org: Multiple: Ajam Media Collective (academic interview), PBS Frontline/Tehran Bureau hosting a Small Media 2012 report (citing a US Broadcasting Board of Governors survey and an Iran state-media poll), Middle East Eye, CSMonitor, Dawn.com (independent press)
time_coverage: 1983-2016 (irregular: bans/legislation dates plus scattered survey years)
frequency: irregular (event dates and scattered survey years, not a continuous series)
units: percent of households/population (ownership/viewership); count (dishes destroyed); minutes per day (viewing time); event flags for legislation dates
license_terms: Academic interview (fair-use excerpting), NGO/press-report factual reporting, and one Iran-state-media-attributed poll figure (labeled per source policy)
notes: This is an Islamic-Republic-era phenomenon (no Pahlavi-era equivalent -- satellite TV did not exist as a mass medium before the 1979 revolution, and VCR technology only arrived in Iran in the late 1970s just before the revolution) so it intentionally sits outside this project's usual Pahlavi-era weighting, per the mission brief's explicit call-out of satellite dish ownership as a notable IRI-era phenomenon worth covering despite the general pre-1979 focus. CONFIDENCE FLAGGED PER ROW: a widely-repeated but SOURCE-UNVERIFIABLE claim ("2.5 million VCRs in Iran by 1993, one VCR per two televisions") surfaced in web-search AI summaries attributed to MIT Press Reader / Blake Atwood's academic work, but the MIT Press Reader page returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch and no independent primary source could be located to confirm the exact figure -- EXCLUDED from this dataset rather than included on unverified secondhand authority, per project no-fabrication rules. If a future session can access the MIT Press Reader page or Atwood's underlying book/thesis directly, this would be a valuable addition. One NCRI-affiliated result (ncr-iran.org, "Iran regime admits over 40% Iranians view satellite TV") surfaced in search results for this topic and was deliberately NOT used per docs/bookkeeping.md source-reliability policy -- the Middle East Eye/CSMonitor/Dawn.com independent-press coverage of the same underlying events was used instead.
